title: Dog Sudden Onset Dragging Hind Legs: Emergency Guide and Causes
meta_desc: If your dog is suddenly dragging their hind legs, this is a high-priority medical emergency. Learn about IVDD, FCE, and immediate steps to take.
content_html:
What is Sudden Hind Leg Paralysis?

Sudden onset of dragging the hind legs, medically referred to as paraparesis or paraplegia, occurs when there is a critical interruption in the communication between the spinal cord and the back limbs. This can be caused by a ruptured disc (IVDD), a spinal stroke (FCE), or acute trauma. It is often accompanied by pain, distress, or a 'knuckling' gait where the dog walks on the tops of their paws.

Is This an Emergency?

Yes. The urgency level for this condition is High. If your dog has lost the ability to use their back legs, they must be seen by a veterinarian or an emergency animal hospital immediately. Rapid intervention is the most significant factor in determining whether a dog will regain the ability to walk. Loss of deep pain sensation is a surgical emergency and every hour counts.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Capturing a brief video of your dog attempting to move can be vital for the veterinary triage team. It provides a visual record of their neurological status at the time of onset, which may change by the time you reach the clinic. A video allows the vet to assess the severity of the motor deficit and prioritize your pet's care the moment you arrive.

faqs.question: Can I wait overnight to see if my dog improves?
faqs.answer: No. Sudden paralysis can become permanent if the pressure on the spinal cord is not addressed immediately. Early diagnosis and treatment offer the best chance for recovery.

faqs.question: What is the most common cause of dragging hind legs?
faqs.answer: Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD) is a leading cause, especially in breeds like Dachshunds, Corgis, and French Bulldogs, though it can happen to any dog.

faqs.question: How should I move my dog to the car?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog as still as possible. Use a flat, firm surface like a piece of plywood or a large, taut blanket as a stretcher to prevent the spine from flexing or twisting.
